coredata.dc:title: Custom-tailoring loose nanocomposite membrane incorporated bipiperidine/graphene quantum dots for high-efficient dye/salt fractionation in hairwork dyeing effluent
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yuefei
Sun, Yueke
Zhang, Nan
Li, Chen
Hou, Mengxiang
Chen, Kexin
Li, Tiemei
coredata.dc:description: The complete fractionating synthetic and real dye/salt mixtures through loose nanofiltration membranes are of great significance for the textile wastewater recycling and reuse. Therefore, in this paper, a custom-tailoring loose nanocomposite membrane was fabricated to validate the feasibility of high-efficient dye/salt fractionation in the real pretreated hairwork dyeing effluent (HDE). It was observed that after partial alternative doping of 4,4′-bipiperidine and continuous incorporating of graphene quantum dots, the resultant membrane skin-layer features including smoothness, hydrophilicity and surface charge as well as nominal pore size were improved synergistically. Consequently, the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane showed outstanding fractionation and antifouling performance for synthetic dye/salt mixtures and real pretreated HDE. Specifically, for the former, the as-prepared TFN-0.2 membrane displayed the highest water permeance of 56.0 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, retention for all selected dyes (≥99.8%) and salt permeability (≥91.2%). Besides, the antifouling indexes in terms of reversible and irreversible fouling ratios of TFN-0.2 membrane against HDE solution reduced from 13.3% and 20.7% of pristine one to 8.32% and 1.34%, respectively. While flux recovery ratio markedly enhanced from 79.1% to 98.5% in the meantime. Overall, this work provides a facile method to fabricate high-efficient loose nanofiltration membranes for sustainably and precisely fractionating dye/salt mixtures from textile wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Loose nanofiltration membrane
Hairwork dyeing effluent
Bipiperidine
Graphene quantum dots
Dye/salt fractionation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the resultant TFC/TFN membranes
Chemical constituents of the selective layers
Mean effective pore size and pore size distribution
Fractionation performance of the resultant TFC/TFN membranes
Antifouling performance of the resultant membranes
Performance comparison with other recent studies on NF membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of BP/GQDs incorporated nanocomposite membranes fabrication processes.
Chemical constituents of the resultant membranes skin layers: (a) ATR-FTIR and (b) XPS wide scan spectra.
FE-SEM images of surface (a-c), cross-sectional (d-f) morphologies and AFM 3D images (h-j) of TFC-0, TFC-0.067 and TFN-0.2 membranes.
PEG rejection (a) and pore size distribution (b) curves of the fabricated TFC-0, TFC-0.067 and TFN-0.2 membranes.
Surface wettabilities and charge properties of the resultant membranes: (a) water contact angle, (b) zeta potential dependence of pH value.

Fractionation performance of the as-prepared loose NF membranes for synthetic dye/salt mixtures (a), UV–vis absorbance of the employed dye solutions before and after treatment by the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane (b).
The changing trends of main ion concentrations (a) and TOC values (b) for real HDE samples fractionated by the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane after dual-stage pretreatment.
Antifouling performance for the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane and its counterpart tested with real HDE in terms of permeate flux and the corresponding FRR, R r and R ir values.
The BP/GQDs incorporated nanocomposite membranes fabricated at different aqueous monomer ratios.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values were obtained under following conditions: operating pressure 6.0 bar, feed temperature 25 °C, pH = 6.5, 100 mg·L−1 direct red 80 dye solution, 500 mg·L−1 NaCl solution

head.ce:title: Custom-tailoring loose nanocomposite membrane incorporated bipiperidine/graphene quantum dots for high-efficient dye/salt fractionation in hairwork dyeing effluent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, School of Environment, Henan Normal University, 46 East of Construction Road, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, School of Environment, Henan Normal University, 46 East of Construction Road, Xinxiang 453007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The complete fractionating synthetic and real dye/salt mixtures through loose nanofiltration membranes are of great significance for the textile wastewater recycling and reuse. Therefore, in this paper, a custom-tailoring loose nanocomposite membrane was fabricated to validate the feasibility of high-efficient dye/salt fractionation in the real pretreated hairwork dyeing effluent (HDE). It was observed that after partial alternative doping of 4,4′-bipiperidine and continuous incorporating of graphene quantum dots, the resultant membrane skin-layer features including smoothness, hydrophilicity and surface charge as well as nominal pore size were improved synergistically. Consequently, the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane showed outstanding fractionation and antifouling performance for synthetic dye/salt mixtures and real pretreated HDE. Specifically, for the former, the as-prepared TFN-0.2 membrane displayed the highest water permeance of 56.0 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, retention for all selected dyes (≥99.8%) and salt permeability (≥91.2%). Besides, the antifouling indexes in terms of reversible and irreversible fouling ratios of TFN-0.2 membrane against HDE solution reduced from 13.3% and 20.7% of pristine one to 8.32% and 1.34%, respectively. While flux recovery ratio markedly enhanced from 79.1% to 98.5% in the meantime. Overall, this work provides a facile method to fabricate high-efficient loose nanofiltration membranes for sustainably and precisely fractionating dye/salt mixtures from textile wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: The hair products produced in Xuchang, China, account for about 35% of the global output. Hairwork dyeing effluent (HDE) is generated from the dyeing and its affiliated rinsing processes located at the back end of the production line, and thus contains not only a larger amount of artificial dyes and other related dyeing auxiliaries and levelling agents, but also several contaminants from wig materials and upstream steps (pickling or neutralization, for instance) [1]. As a result of high water consuming and heavily polluting processes, HDE is typically characterized by high concentration of chromaticity (3200 times), turbidity (150 NTU), COD (2140 mg·L−1), salinity (2.36‰), low biodegradability and several toxic dyestuffs [2]. In addition, it should be noted that the types and concentrations of pollutants contained in HDE often vary with the actual demand of the product, resulting in complex composition and large fluctuation in water quality, which further brings great difficulties for its deep treatment [3,4]. In our previous work, an environment-friendly cellulose-based flocculant (CLF-10) was modified and utilized to pretreat HDE coupled with inexpensive ultrafiltration (UF) membrane, yielding a high filtrate recovery of 98% and satisfied gray-water quality only involving abundant small-sized organic dyes and intact salts [5]. From the perspective of cleaner production, fractionating dye/salt mixtures from such textile wastewater towards valuable material recycling is very necessary, and has garnered widespread attention in recent years [6–9].

In efforts to fractionate textile wastewater, several methods have been adopted ranging from biological degradation, adsorption, advanced oxidation and coagulation [10]. Nevertheless, these methods have disadvantages such as time-consuming, exclusively focusing on dye destruction, not easy to scale up, introducing many secondary contaminants, and thus are not adequate to meet increasingly stringent environmental restrictions [6,11]. In this case, loose nanofiltration membrane (LNM) was given an important role based on its own unique capabilities such as relatively high water flux, salt permeability and dyes rejection as well as low energy consumption [12–15]. To date, LNMs have been exploited via various preparation methods, mainly including phase inversion, surface coating and interfacial polymerization. Closely related to this is the gradually developing of numerous new strategies, such as using electrospinning or forward pressure-assisted assembly, taking advantage of intrinsic microporous polymer and so on [16,17]. Also, some efforts have been made to gain such functional layers for LNMs via doping longer or twisted reactive monomers, inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) into the casting solution, to precisely regulate the steric-hindrance effect [18,19]. For example, Li and coworkers firstly synthesized the long-chain amino monomer in aqueous phase by Michael addition reaction with the participation of tannic acid, and thus prepared a LNM achieving impressive performance in dye/salt fractionation (32.6 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 of flux, 97.5%, only 9.41% and 2.55% of Congo Red, Na2SO4 and NaCl retention, respectively) [19]. Interestingly, by taking advantage of fouling to reversely shrink UF substrate pore size, Shen et al. also fabricated a LNM and successfully applied in the fractionation of dyes/sodium chloride mixture [20]. Besides, Tavangar et al. confirmed that both nominal pore size and porosity of the CeO2-loaded polyethersulfone LNM increased apparently by 96.9% and 14.7% when CeO2 content increased from zero to 2.0 wt%, probably due to instantaneous demixing during the preparation [10]. In return, this modified membrane showed good removal of dyes and transmission of salts in reusing textile wastewater.

Beyond these, from the point of view of electrostatic interaction, several studies concluded that preparing electroneutral or opposite-charged LNMs with the penetrated di-/multi- valent ions seem to be good options for fractionating salt-containing dye wastewater [21–23]. It is generally believed that these two kinds of LNMs might promote the above-mentioned bottleneck ions permeation by reducing the charge exclusion effect or even with the aid of electrostatic attraction. For instance, the loose electroneutral zinc incorporated polymer network nanocomposite membrane was reported to exhibit improved selectivity for dye/salt separation (greater than99.7% and 3–14% for their rejection rates, respectively) and moderately high water permeance of 14.5 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 [23]. However, it should also be noted regrettably that electroneutral LNMs lack further help from the Donnan effect in retaining the dissociated dyestuffs, and compromise the resulting decrease in membrane surface hydrophilicity and permeability. While tunable charged LNMs are suitable only for such influent poor/rich in oppositely charged dyes and di-/multi- valent ions. In brief, the synergistic effects between pore size and charge performance on the perm-selectivity of the resultant membrane as well as the specific influent properties should be taken into account together when modifying LNMs. Furthermore, the relevant anti-fouling studies reported less, and many fractionation processes merely involve simulated multi-component saline dye wastewater without clear industry positioning.

As mentioned above, the physicochemical properties of HDE showed obvious fluctuation with order forms and were closely related to the types of colouring, dyeing auxiliary and levelling agents as well as residues from upstream processes. It was unexpectedly found that when hydrochloric acid rather than sulfuric acid reagent was added in the pickling process and other levelling agents rather than Glauber’s salt (sodium sulfate) were used in the dyeing process, inorganic salts of HDE obtained after preoptimized dual-stage pretreatment were mainly composed of plentiful monovalent ions (Na+, Cl−, NH4 +, NO3 −) and particular divalent cation (Ca2+). Moreover, raw HDE showed higher negative zeta potential of −26.3 mV due to its rich in a variety of colloids and dyes [5]. Given all this, LNMs modified based on the aforesaid pore size regulation strategy and with a negative surface charge are very suitable for high-efficient fractionation of dye/salt in HDE.

Herein, both axisymmetry-structured 4,4′-bipiperidine (BP) allowing chain extension and 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were selected as a long-chain amino monomer and NPs, respectively, and incorporated alternatively and continuously during interfacial polymerization (IP) of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). In this context, apart from finely regulating steric-hindrance and electrostatic effects, the additional high permeability and fouling resistance of the fabricated loose nanocomposite membrane brought by GQDs doping are also expected in fractionating the synthetic dye/salt mixtures and real HDE. The influences of BP and GQDs incorporation on skin features of the resultant LNMs separation layers were clarified in detail. Also, the improved perm-selectivity performance was assessed using single-component (numerous dyes and electrolytes) solutions and real HDE collected from the local sources followed by antifouling behavior during long-term running, and compared to other published thin-film composite (TFC)/nanocomposite (TFN) membranes.

Flat-sheet polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membrane with an average molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 40 kDa was brought from Daona Membrane Factory and used as a substrate (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA), TMC, PIP, sodium phosphate tribasic dodecahydrate (Na3PO4·12H2O), neutral polyethylene glycol (PEG) with numerous molecular weights (200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 Da) and dye solutes including direct black 38, direct red 80 and alcian blue 8GX (main physicochemical properties shown in Table S1) were purchased from Aladdin Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). BP was supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), n-hexane, anhydrous ethanol, sodium hydroxide and inorganic salts (NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2) were offered by Sinopharm Reagent Company (Beijing, China). All of the above mentioned analytical grade chemicals were used as received without further purification. Besides, the real HDE after flocculation-sedimentation and UF pretreatment (DOC = 41.1 mg·L−1, total dissolved solids (TDS) = 2400 mg·L−1) [5] was also selected to evaluate the high-efficient fractionation performance of the prepared LNMs. Deionized (DI) water (3.8 μS·cm−1) was adopted throughout this research.

GQDs employed in this work were synthesized by direct pyrolysis of CA from a bottom-up method [24], as follows. Firstly, 3.5 g of CA was put into a crucible and heated to 200 °C for 35 min. During this period, CA was firstly liquated to colorless and then changed to orange, suggesting the generation of GQDs. Subsequently, after adding dropwise into 150 mL of 8.0 mg·mL−1 NaOH aqueous solution immediately with vigorous magnetic stirring, the obtained solution was put into a dialysis bag with a MWCO of 1500 Da and dialyzed in DI water for 2 days to remove excess CA. Thereafter, this solution in the bag was freeze-dried to gain solid GQDs.

All BP/GQDs modified loose nanocomposite membranes were fabricated on PES substrates through IP method, as shown in Fig. 1 . First of all, PIP and BP with different mass ratios and GQDs with different contents were dissolved in 2.0 wt% Na3PO4·12H2O buffer aqueous solution, which are given in Table 1 . Then, the pretreated PES membrane was taped to a glass plate and immersed into the aqueous phase for 5 min. After that, the residual aqueous solution was removed from PES substrate skin layer using filter papers. Next, the saturated UF membrane was immersed into an organic phase of 0.1 wt% TMC in n-hexane for 2 min, resulting in the generation of a polyamide functional layer. Finally, excess organic phase was drained and the resultant membranes were air-dried at 60 °C for 15 min for further polymerization reaction. In particular, 0.067 wt% and 0.2 g·L−1 are identified as the optimal doping content of BP and GQDs for preparing TFC/TFN membranes on the basis of their perm-selectivity orthogonal experimental results as presented in Table 1.

Chemical structures of the synthesized GQDs nanoparticles and prepared membranes active layers were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, America), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NEXUS, America) and attenuated total reflection infrared spectroscopy (ATR-IR, Spectrum 400, America), respectively. Size distribution and surface morphologies of these GQDs nanoparticles were detected by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Nano ZS90, UK), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, MultiMode 8, America), respectively. Surface morphologies of these LNMs were detected by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU8010, Japan) at an accelerated voltage of 5 kV. The samples were sputter-coated with gold before observation. AFM was utilized to further detect their surface roughnesses. Each membrane was pre-dried in vacuum for 24 h and scanned in the range of 2 µm × 2 µm in the scanasyst mode. Contact angle goniometer (CA, theta, Sweden) was employed for determination of surface wettabilities of the resultant membranes using water droplets. According to double-electric layer theory, zeta potential on each NF membrane surface was gained indirectly by adopting a home-made bench-scale tangential streaming potential analyzer and 1 mmol·L−1 KCl solution at 25 °C [25].

A series of neutral PEGs retention measurements with constant concentration of 200 mg·L−1 were carried out to measure the resultant membrane pore size distribution and determined by total organic carbon analyzer (Vario TOC, Elementar, Germany). In general, the relationship between stokes diameter (d p) of a hypothetical neutral solute and its molecular weight (M w) could be expressed as the following equation [26].
(1)
d p
= 33.5 ×
10
- 3
M w
0.557

Hence, nominal pore size was gained from the MWCO curve when PEG rejection of the membrane attained 90%. When the influence of steric and hydrodynamic interaction between solute and pores on solute removal was ignored, the pore size distribution of the fabricated membrane could be described by the following probability density function [27]:
(2)
d R
d p
d
d p
=
1
d p
ln
σ p
2 π
exp
-
ln
d p
- ln
μ p
2
2
ln
σ p
2
where the average effective pore size (µ p) was equivalent to d p determined at R = 50%, and the geometric standard deviation (σ p) was identified as the ratio of d p at R = 84.1% and 50%.

Fractionation performance in terms of water permeance and dye/salt rejection of the resultant NF membranes was evaluated using a tangential-flow filtration NF device with an effective membrane area of 28.3 cm2. The experiment was conducted under constant operating pressure of 6.0 bar, feed temperature of 25 ± 0.3 °C and cross-flow velocity of 0.072 m·s−1 for 500 mg·L−1 aforementioned electrolyte solutions, 100 mg·L−1 organic dye solutions and the real HDE after two-stage pretreatment in sequence. Concentrations of dyes and salts as well as inorganic ions before and after permeation were measured by UV-spectrophotometer (UV-3600Plus, Shimadzu, Japan), conductivity meter (FE30K, Mettler, China) and ion chromatography (AQUION, Thermo Fisher, America), respectively. Water flux (J) and solute rejection (R) were calculated by the ratio of indirectly measured permeate flow rate to effective membrane area and operating pressure (L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) and the percentage of dividing the rejected solute concentration by the solute content in feed (%), respectively.

Besides, the real pretreated HDE was continued to be used in the antifouling assessment for 2 cycles involving the unmodified (TFC-0) and optimal (TFN-0.2) membranes. Firstly, each pristine membrane was pre-compacted and filtrated with DI water for 8 h to gain a stable initial pure water flux (J 0), and then DI water was replaced by HDE solution to perform the fouling test lasted for additional 32 h to gain permeate flux (J p). Next, physical cleaning was implemented with DI water for final 8 h, followed by pure water permeability (J final) measurement again. Thus, fouling resistance index comprising the flux recovery ratio (FRR), reversible fouling ratio (R r), irreversible fouling ratio (R ir) of the membranes could be obtained according to the previous work [25].

Physicochemical properties of the synthesized GQDs were comprehensively characterized, as shown in Fig. S1. Thereinto, TEM image of GQDs (see Fig. S1a) demonstrated the well-dispersed spherical point-like morphology with a narrow size distribution between 1 and 7 nm. Similarly, DLS measurement depicted in Fig. S1b was highly consistent with TEM size distribution result, and the acquired average diameter of the as-prepared GQDs was 2.9 nm. Meanwhile, AFM tests seen in Figs. S1(c–d) also verified the spherical point-like topography of GQDs, which were approximately 0.58 nm in height, meaning that the vast majority of GQDs contain only 1–2 graphene layers [24]. In addition, surface chemical nature of GQDs (displayed in Figs. S1(e–f)) was characterized by FTIR and XPS, respectively. Specifically, characteristic absorption peaks at around 3300 cm−1 and 1551 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH group and asymmetric stretching vibration of COOH group. XPS wide scan analysis indicated that the oxygen content reached up to 37.2%. Both the ultra-fine size and abundant hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups endowed the synthesized GQDs with anticipated modification potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the resultant TFC/TFN membranes
Chemical constituents of the selective layers
ce:section.ce:para: ATR-FTIR spectra of PES substrate, bare TFC-0, TFC-0.067 and TFN-0.2 membranes are illustrated in Fig. 2 a. Compared with the spectrum of PES supporting layer, new peaks at 1740 and 1366 cm−1 were detected in the spectra of TFC-0, TFC-0.067 and TFN-0.2 membranes, which is attributed to the CO stretching, CN bending vibrations in amide groups, confirming the successful formation of polyamide layer on the PES substrate through interfacial polymerization [28]. Moreover, in the spectrum of TFN-0.2 membrane, after deducting the factor that the peak for the NH is overlapped, the broad band at about 3440 cm−1 represents OH stretching vibration, which may be caused by the incorporation of GQDs. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2b, XPS was employed to further analyze their near-surface chemical compositions (N 1s and O 1s core-level spectra see Fig. S2). Compared with both TFC membranes, contribution of O 1s for TFN-0.2 membrane increased significantly, validating again the successful introduction of oxygen-rich GQDs. More impressive, the N/O ratio of TFC-0.067 membrane dropped obviously from 0.785 of TFC-0 membrane to 0.568 after incorporating BP, indicating that the chemical crosslinking degree of polyamide active layer decreased [29]. This change is related to the chemical structure of BP molecule itself. BP consists of two piperidine units that bind to each other through CC bond with a larger spatial size than PIP, resulting in a reduction of reactive sites per unit reaction area, thus bringing about a loose polyamide structure. After adding GQDs, the N/O ratio of the TFN-0.2 membrane further decreased to 0.259. This may be because the hydration repulsion forces for GQDs hinder their firm fixation with polyamide macromolecules, forming the interface space and obtaining the loose polyamide structure [30].

Morphologies of the fabricated LNMs selective layers incorporated BP/GQDs were evaluated by FE-SEM and AFM, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 3 . A dense polyamide layer with some globules and nodules was visualized on the surface of pristine TFC-0 membrane, deriving from typical interfacial polymerization between PIP and TMC. In contrast, after doping modification, TFC-0.067 and TFN-0.2 membranes exhibited relatively inconspicuous nodules structure with smoother surface. And this tendency was moderately strengthened with the increase of BP/GQDs content (see Fig. S3). This is probably mainly because the doping of BP and GQDs reduces the crosslinking rate in IP reaction (e.g. the steric hindrance effect for the former and the dragging effect of the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds for the latter), which leads to the formation of smaller convex structure and smoother membrane surface [31]. Fig. 3(d–f) show the cross-sectional morphologies of these selected membranes. It could be seen that there is no significant difference among their skin-layer thicknesses all around 160 nm, indicating that the addition of BP and GQDs will not directly influence the membrane functional layer thickness. AFM test was also performed to confirm the surface roughness of each as-prepared TFC/TFN membrane. Fig. 3(h–j) display the 3D AFM images of the as-prepared membranes and the corresponding surface root mean square roughness (Rms) values. In accordance with FE-SEM results, Rms values of TFC-0, TFC-0.067 and TFN-0.2 membranes successively decreased from 6.70 nm to 6.10 nm and 5.51 nm. Besides the constrained crosslinking rate factor mentioned earlier, TFN-0.2 membrane with the smoothest morphology could also be attributed to plentiful zero-dimensional GQDs locating at the membrane surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mean effective pore size and pore size distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The probability density function curves, MWCOs and mean effective pore sizes of these membranes are shown in Fig. 4 . It could be found that TFC-0 membrane possesses a low nominal pore size of 0.78 nm and a small MWCO of 534 Da. After doping BP, the pore size of TFC-0.067 membrane increased to 0.91 nm and the corresponding MWCO enlarged to 791 Da, which manifests that partial alternative doping of BP containing lower reactive amine group density than PIP during the IP reaction forms a relatively loose and nanoporous polyamide structure [32]. Moreover, the addition of GQDs further increases the nominal pore size. The average effective pore size of TFN-0.2 membrane becomes 1.07 nm with a MWCO of 1107 Da, which is ascribed to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between GQDs and PIP/BP to slow down the interfacial polymerization reaction rate [33]. The larger pores and more hydrophilic surface of TFN-0.2 membrane will be more beneficial to the passage of water molecules, thereby achieving the purpose of improving permeate flux and reducing membrane fouling.

Water CAs and zeta potentials dependence of pH value of the resultant TFC/TFN membranes functional layers are depicted in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5a, after partial alternative doping of BP and continuous incorporating of GQDs, the measured CA at 20 s decreased slightly from 45.0° for pristine TFC-0 membrane to 44.7° for TFC-0.067 membrane and markedly to 36.8° for TFN-0.2 membrane, respectively. This echoes the results of the pore size distribution, as both modified membranes becoming looser, leading to more hydrophilic membrane surface. Notably, TFN-0.2 membrane exhibited a quite lower water CA compared with TFC-0.067 membrane, which was probably ascribed to the addition of hydrophilic GQDs. For one thing, abundant polar oxygen-containing functional groups in GQDs endowed TFN-0.2 membrane surface with better wettability. For another, due to the interference effect of the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between GQDs and PIP/BP, the cross-linking degree of PA functional layer decreased [30,34], which leads to more unreactive acyl chloride groups remaining on TFN-0.2 membrane surface. Both favorable factors contributed to the significantly improvement for the optimal membrane hydrophilicity, although the reduced membrane surface roughness (see AFM data in Fig. 3) was not conducive to this variation in the process. For the same reason, it could be observed that with the prolonging of time, CA of TFN-0.2 membrane in this context declined more remarkably. With regard to surface charge properties, as shown from the typical zeta potential curves in Fig. 5b, the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane was found to have a more negatively-charged surface than those of bare TFC-0 and TFC-0.067 membranes at the same inlet water pH, let alone PES substrate in the absence of active layer. As mentioned before, in addition to the doping GQDs with rich carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups, the associated decrease in the cross-linking degree of PA layer also results in more polyamide network terminals and prompts more dominant acyl chloride functional groups to remain on the membrane surface [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fractionation performance of the resultant TFC/TFN membranes

ce:section.ce:para: Both three types of inorganic salts and organic dyes were employed to evaluate the fractionation performance of the resultant loose TFC/TFN membranes, as shown in Fig. 6 . It is clear from Fig. 6a that all membranes have similar high rejection rates of more than 99.8% for various organic dyes but quite different permeate fluxes. Specifically, water flux increased markedly with the progression of modification steps. Here, the TFC-0 membrane exhibited the lowest permeate flux of 35.4, 36.1 and 35.3 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 for direct black 38, direct red 80 and alcian blue 8GX aqueous solution, respectively, compared to 49.6, 49.9 and 49.6 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 for those of the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane. The increased permeate flux could be explained from the following aspects. Firstly, due to the quantum size, good dispersion and smooth surface structure of GQDs, not only more water channels could be formed in the PA layer not easy to generate non-selective defects, but also water molecules can pass through the channels quickly [29,35]. Secondly, the incorporation of GQDs improves the resultant membrane surface hydrophilicity, which will promote the solubilization of water molecules in TFN-0.2 membrane, thus also improving water permeability [36,37]. Thirdly, the intramolecular hydrogen bonding interactions between the polar oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs and water molecules provide an extra driving force for water transport within the water channels, which immensely enhances the water permeability [33,38]. Fourthly, the nominal pore size of TFN-0.2 membrane increases by 0.29 nm through continuous doping of BP and GQDs, which is also helpful to the rapid passage of water molecules. Consequently, TFN-0.2 membrane possesses the highest flux without sacrificing dye removal efficiencies, with the aid of stronger electrostatic repulsion to targeted contaminants derived from the more negatively charged surface than its counterparts (see Fig. 5b). The almost completely faded colours of the relevant actual permeate samples for the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane depicted in Fig. 6b also verified the point of view. In contrast, for inorganic ions, TFC-0 and TFC-0.067 membranes displayed the relatively higher rejection rates of 26.7%, 22.5%, 14.2%, 9.61% and 18.8%, 18.5%, 12.6%, 6.48% for Cl−, Mg2+, Na+ and Ca2+, respectively, compared to 9.85%, 9.65%, 8.44% and 1.39% of the TFN-0.2 membrane. Meanwhile, the average permeate flux of the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane was enlarged from 39.8 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 for neat membrane to 56.0 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1. To sum up, according to the above results, it could be concluded that the novel loose TFN-0.2 membrane exhibited enormous potential in high-efficient fractionating dye/salt mixtures from textile effluent towards minimizing the environmental impact of hazardous dyestuff.

To further verify whether the real HDE could be treated with the same high separating performance, the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane was selected and used for testing. Fig. 7 displays the changing trends of main ion concentrations and TOC values for real HDE samples fractionated by the modified loose TFN-0.2 membrane after pre-optimized dual-stage pretreatment employing both home-made CLF-10 and UF membrane (HM90) [5]. Compared with raw HDE, the concentrations of main inorganic salts in CLF-10 supernatant and UF filtrate were almost unchanged. More impressive, as a result of electrostatic and steric effects, the concentrations of all inorganic ions after NF permeation only dropped slightly and their descending sequences were in the order of: NO3 − (8.94%) > Cl− (8.86%) > Na+ (7.60%) > Ca2+ (5.84%) > NH4 + (5.09%). While organics rejection calculated by TOC after each processing unit declined continuously and significantly. Specifically, this value decreased from 231 mg·L−1 for raw HDE to 69.5 mg·L−1 for CLF-10 supernatant, 44.2 mg·L−1 for UF filtrate, and ultimately 3.2 mg·L−1 for NF permeate, respectively. Even if only individual NF processing unit was considered, it was also found that the TOC removal efficiency was as high as 92.8%. These results confirm that the distinct advantages in terms of outstanding salt permeability and dyes rejection render TFN-0.2 membrane a promising material for the selective separation of real salt-containing dye wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling performance of the resultant membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The antifouling properties of TFC-0 and TFN-0.2 membranes were studied using pretreated HDE solution and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 8 . Obviously, the incorporation of GQDs did enhance the antifouling performance of TFN-0.2 membrane against HDE solution. It has been manifested principally in two facets. Firstly, compared with pristine membrane, the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane always ran at a higher level of permeate flux during a 2-cycle fouling experiment, and the attenuation degree of this value was significantly lower at the end of the fouling test. Secondly, after physical flushing, the recovered permeate flux for TFN-0.2 membrane tended to approach its initial value more easily. In detail, the antifouling indexes in terms of R r and R ir of TFN-0.2 membrane decreased from 13.3% and 20.7% of neat one to 8.32% and 1.34%, respectively. While FRR dramatically increased from 79.1% to 98.5% during the period. These data indicated that the successful introduction of GQDs significantly enhanced the resistance to HDE fouling for TFN-0.2 membrane, which might be ascribed to the improved surface hydrophilicity, electronegativity and decreased surface roughness. Primarily, the addition of GQDs strengthened binding ability to water molecules both within and around the PA layer and facilitated the generation of hydration layer on the membrane surface to resist fouling [39,40]. Meanwhile, the doping of GQDs led to a smoother membrane surface due to the atomically smooth surface of graphene sheets, which weakened the adhesion of foulants on membrane surface and hence reduced the non-specific adsorption [24,41]. Furthermore, the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane exhibited higher electronegativity than TFC-0 membrane, which enhanced the repulsion between membrane surface and organic foulants with negative charge, thereby improving the fouling resistance of the resultant membrane surface. In summary, TFN-0.2 membrane has excellent antifouling and stable long-term (see Fig. S4) performance for high-efficient dye/salt fractionation in HDE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison with other recent studies on NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To compare dye/salt fractionation and antifouling performance of the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane with other recent NF membranes, the relevant experimental data were comprehensively collected in Table 2 . Clearly, the as-prepared TFN-0.2 membrane exhibited outstanding dye/salt fractionation performance in terms of high water permeance of 59.6 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, high rejection for dyes (≥99.8%) and low salt retention (≤8.8%) as well as good antifouling ability (98.5% of FRR), which were superior than those of most other NF membranes. Moreover, the dye removal rates calculated by TOC value and salt permeability of the modified TFN-0.2 membrane to real HDE were as high as 92.8% and larger than 91.1%, respectively. Overall, the prepared novel loose TFN-0.2 membrane in this work exhibited high-efficient dye/salt fractionation performance, which indicates that it has great potential for real dyeing effluent treatment.

In this study, the feasibility of efficiently fractionating synthetic and real dye/salt mixtures was verified by a custom-tailoring loose nanocomposite membrane incorporated BP/GQDs. Compared with the neat membrane skin-layer features, partial alternative doping of axisymmetry-structured BP allowing chain extension could only significantly increase the nominal pore size and reduce the crosslinking degree of PA layer, thereby leading to the generation of smoother membrane surface. Nevertheless, in addition to further reinforcing the above positive effects, continuous incorporating of GQDs also endowed the prepared TFN membrane with markedly improved hydrophilicity and charge property. As a consequence, the optimal TFN-0.2 membrane exhibited superior fractionation and antifouling performance for synthetic and real dye/salt mixtures. Specifically, the as-prepared TFN-0.2 membrane displayed the highest water permeance of 56.0 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, retention for all selected dyes (≥99.8%) and salt permeability (≥91.2%) tested by aqueous dye/salt solutions. Besides, during the fouling experiment lasted for 84 h, the antifouling indexes in terms of R r and R ir of TFN-0.2 membrane against HDE solution reduced from 13.3% and 20.7% of bare one to 8.32% and 1.34%, respectively. While FRR prominently increased from 79.1% to 98.5% during the period. Overall, the novel modified loose TFN-0.2 membrane in this research shows great potential in precisely and sustainably fractionating dye/salt mixtures from textile wastewater towards minimizing the environmental impact of hazardous dyestuff.

Yuefei Song: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Yueke Sun: Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. Nan Zhang: Data curation. Chen Li: Investigation. Mengxiang Hou: . Kexin Chen: . Tiemei Li: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Henan provincial key science and technology research project (No. 192102210045, 202102310611).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.118870.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
